Weaving starts with a single thread, pulled taut across the loom. Every
pattern, no matter how complex, is built from thousands of these threads
crossing at right angles.

The weaver's grandmother used to say that a good thread is judged not by
its color but by how evenly it holds tension across the whole cloth.

Some looms use a shuttle to carry the thread back and forth; others rely
on a more modern channel that feeds thread automatically, freeing the
weaver's hands for finer work.
